Wind Advisory through late Mon night for Kent County: NW winds 15–30 mph with gusts up to ~55 mph; can cause blowing snow and isolated power issues.

meteorology.overall_conditions_summary: For Rockford (Kent Co.) on Tue 2026-03-17: temperatures stay well below freezing (mid-teens to low 20s), but the *meaningful precip is minimal* in your hourly breakdown—only a brief burst of light snow around 12 AM (~0.1" total). After 1 AM it’s dry with improving visibility and gradually easing winds. The biggest operational issue is cold + wind: wind chills around -0°F to +2°F during 6–8 AM bus time (NOT at the -15°F to -20°F danger/auto-closure thresholds). With no accumulating snow/ice during the morning commute and several hours for crews to clean up anything left from Monday’s advisory-event, a Michigan district would very likely run on time. Snow-day/closure probability: low (~5–10%); delay probability: low to modest (~10–20%) mainly if residual slick spots/drifting persist from Monday night into early Tuesday on untreated back roads.

similar_weather_patterns.pattern_description: Late-season (mid‑March) post‑frontal cold shot with lingering light snow/flurries overnight, then rapid drying/clearing by daybreak with temps staying well below freezing. In Rockford/Kent County this is a common ‘wraparound/lake-enhanced’ tail-end setup: a quick burst overnight, then plows can catch up before buses.

Light glazing/freezing-drizzle threat advertised in the daily summary, but hourly temperatures remain solidly below freezing and the hour-by-hour precip signal is tiny and ends very early. In Michigan, these ‘headline says freezing rain but amounts are trace and done before 3–5 AM’ cases usually do NOT close—unless there’s verified glaze on untreated back roads/bridges at 6–7 AM.

Wind-driven snow event the prior day (Mon 2026-03-16) leaving some drifting/patchy slick spots overnight, followed by colder temps that ‘lock in’ whatever slush remains. Historically this can create isolated rural-route problems even when new snowfall is negligible.

seasonal_context.typical_conditions_for_date: For Rockford (west of Grand Rapids) by mid‑March, schools and road crews are still fully winter-capable, but the *decision threshold* for closing nudges a bit higher than early winter because districts are used to variability and often try to stay open unless there’s clear widespread hazard. A small overnight snow (≈0–1 inch) with improving conditions toward sunrise is typically a ‘school day’ outcome.
unusual_factors: Temperatures are notably cold for mid‑March (teens to low 20s °F), which increases the chance that any moisture becomes persistent black ice on untreated surfaces.

Forecast text flags 'light freezing rain' with high daily chance of rain (84%), but the hour-by-hour data shows essentially all measurable precip concentrated around 12–1 AM and then dry through the bus window—this mismatch raises uncertainty about whether there’s an unmodeled drizzle/freezing drizzle risk near dawn.
Monday features strong winds/advisories and periods of blowing snow; if that produces localized drifting or a thin polished layer, Tuesday morning can start slick even without new accumulation.

local_microclimates: US‑131 corridor and more urbanized surfaces near Rockford/Grand Rapids tend to get treated earlier and stay more passable; rural connectors and shaded north-facing segments can stay icy longer.
Low-lying areas near the Rogue River and small creek valleys can trap cold air overnight; bridges and overpasses freeze first and stay slick in the morning commute window.
If winds remain WNW/NW overnight, any leftover loose snow from Monday can drift into open-country stretches (less of a city issue, more of an outlying bus-route issue).
infrastructure_considerations: Kent County districts generally have strong plow/salt capacity and experienced winter bus operations; closures usually require either (a) significant snow during 5–8 AM, (b) verified icing/glaze, or (c) widespread drifting/near-whiteout.
Because the precip ends very early (around 12–1 AM in the hourly feed), crews have a long runway (roughly 5–7+ hours) to treat and clear before buses.
location_specific_factors.elevation_impact: Rockford’s modest elevation changes won’t create mountain-style issues, but small hills plus untreated secondary roads can amplify slip risk if there’s even a light glaze. The bigger elevation-related factor is bridges/overpasses and exposed hilltops cooling faster in the teens.

history.confidence_assessment: Overall closure signal for Tue 2026-03-17 is LOW to MODEST. New snow amounts are near-trace (≈0.1 inch) and the measurable precip is forecast to end shortly after midnight, which in Michigan historically corresponds to a low full-closure rate (~5–15%). The main ‘wild card’ is whether the 'light freezing rain' label represents a real pre-dawn freezing drizzle/glaze not reflected in the hourly breakdown; true icing (even light) can push closure probability sharply upward. Calibrated estimate for Rockford: ~10% chance of a full snow day, ~20% chance of a delay, and ~70% normal schedule—confidence: medium (because of the freezing-rain vs hourly-precip mismatch).

safety_recommendations: Main hazard is *residual slick spots/black ice* from Monday’s wintry mix + overnight refreeze. Have transportation staff do a 5:00–5:30 AM test drive on known problem spots (bridges/overpasses, shaded curves, hill routes, and rural connectors).

If any freezing drizzle redevelops (even “light”), prioritize salting of building approaches: bus loops, staff/parent drop-off, crosswalks, sidewalks, and steps—these become the injury hotspot even when roads look “fine.”
Bus ops: run normal routes but allow extra stopping distance and slightly reduced speeds on subdivision streets; be ready for spot delays on less-traveled roads that may be glazed.

Communications: message families to expect a cold, breezy wait at stops (wind chills near/around 0°F early). Encourage full winter gear and earlier-than-usual stop arrival to reduce time standing in the wind.

Decision trigger for escalation: if early-morning observations show *active freezing drizzle* or widespread glazing on untreated surfaces, shift quickly toward a delay/closure despite low precip totals—ice is the equalizer in Michigan.

This same advisory narrative highlights outage risk from wind (tree limbs down), which can factor into closure decisions if outages become widespread overnight.

news.key_findings_summary: As of Monday 2026-03-16, I did not locate an official Rockford Public Schools (Rockford, MI) announcement confirming a closure/delay for Tuesday 2026-03-17 on the district homepage. However, a National Weather Service Grand Rapids Winter Weather Advisory for Kent County (circulating via Reddit) calls for 2–5 inches of snow plus strong wind gusts (~45 mph) through Tuesday late morning, specifically flagging hazardous Tuesday AM commute conditions. Separately, statewide outage tracking shows a notable number of Michigan customers without power with Consumers Energy heavily impacted, which—if concentrated in Kent County overnight—can become a meaningful closure signal. I did not find confirmed closure announcements for neighboring districts in this pass, and WOODTV closings pages were blocked by robots.txt from this tool, limiting direct verification from that outlet.

state_highways.estimated_clear_time: 2-4 hours after steadier snow ends (likely overnight). Expect improving to mostly clear/bare by around 6:30 AM if precip ends near/after midnight and temps stay ~15–17°F.

county_roads.estimated_clear_time: 2-4 hours after snow ends for primary county routes/bus corridors; secondary county roads may still be slick at 6:30 AM.

local_streets.estimated_clear_time: 4-6+ hours for residential streets; many subdivisions may be rutted/packed early Tuesday.

parking_lots.estimated_clear_time: Most school lots typically start clearing very early (often 4:30–6:30 AM). With cold temps, packed snow/ice will linger even after plowing.

clearing_timeline.snow_end_time: Most impactful wintry precip for Rockford looks to taper late Monday evening into around midnight Tuesday (your hourly shows a final light burst at 12:00 AM Tue, then dry).
clearing_timeline.estimated_road_condition_at_6_30_am: State highways (US-131/I-96 corridors) should be plowed/salted and largely passable; county primaries mostly passable but slick; residential/local streets likely still snow-packed in spots, especially where drifting occurred Monday with strong NW winds.

infrastructure.overall_clearing_assessment: For Tuesday 2026-03-17, the *amount* of new snow overnight is small (~0.3 cm / ~0.1 in in your Tuesday day bucket, with the last meaningful hour at 12:00 AM), but the bigger operational issue is: (1) residual snow/blowing/drifting from Monday’s event, (2) very cold temps (mid-teens) limiting melt, and (3) potential refreeze/black-ice risk after crews treat. MDOT trunklines should be in good shape by the morning bus window; the main risk for bus routes is untreated/partly-treated neighborhood connectors and subdivision streets that remain packed/slick. This setup leans more toward 'roads are passable but slippery' rather than 'roads are impassable'.

powerGrid.overall_grid_assessment: For Tuesday (2026-03-17) in Rockford (Kent County): weather is cold (low ~15°F) with wind chills near/around 0°F overnight and a freezing-rain signal in the day-level forecast. From a power-infrastructure perspective, the biggest concern is not generation shortfall but distribution damage risk (trees/branches) and the consequence of even a modest local outage: buildings lose heat quickly and can’t be occupied until warmed back up. Statewide, outages are currently elevated and dominated by Consumers Energy customers, but without a Kent County/Rockford-specific outage count and ETRs, power conditions are a wildcard rather than a decisive closure trigger. Net: give power-grid factors some weight (+10–15%) only if you confirm meaningful outages in north Kent/Rockford overnight; otherwise roads/ice will dominate the snow-day decision.

final.decision_rationale:
Across meteorology/history/safety/infrastructure, the base case is a classic West Michigan “storm Monday, cleanup Tuesday” setup: the provided hourly forecast concentrates the last measurable snow around midnight with improving visibility thereafter, leaving ~6.5 hours for plows/salt before buses. Michigan districts usually stay open in that scenario, especially with only ~0.1" new snow indicated.

Where the experts meaningfully disagree is not snowfall totals but *second-order hazards*: (1) advisory language through late morning + strong winds can keep drifting/slippery conditions going even after snowfall ends, (2) cold mid-teen temps increase black-ice persistence on untreated neighborhood streets and bridges, and (3) a mismatch between ‘light freezing rain’ labeling/high daily precip chance vs mostly-dry hourly data leaves a non-zero risk of trace freezing drizzle/glaze near dawn. Those factors raise the tail risk—but without confirmed neighboring-district closures and without confirmed Rockford/Kent power outages, the most Brier-optimal call remains **OPEN with monitoring**, with **a meaningful chance of a 2-hour delay** if early-morning spot checks find widespread glaze/drifting on bus-route connectors or if any school loses heat overnight.

Extreme-cold auto-closure thresholds are NOT triggered: the web verifier found no credible ≤ -20°F wind chill in the 6–8 AM window (API ~0°F; web snippets even warmer).

alternative_scenarios.scenario: Most likely: Open on time (cleanup day). Roads passable on trunklines/primaries; isolated slick subdivision streets but manageable with caution.
alternative_scenarios.impact: Normal schedule; minor bus delays on a few routes; higher slip risk on untreated sidewalks/parking areas.

alternative_scenarios.scenario: Delay scenario: 2-hour delay due to patchy black ice/drifting on neighborhood connectors and hills, or a localized power/heat issue at one or more buildings.
alternative_scenarios.impact: Gives time for additional salting, daylight improvement, and building re-heat if power flickers overnight.

alternative_scenarios.scenario: Worst case: Full closure if pre-dawn freezing drizzle creates a thin glaze widely, or if notable outages in Rockford/north Kent persist into morning preventing building heat/operations.
alternative_scenarios.impact: Districtwide safety/operations issue despite low new snowfall totals.

for_schools: Recommendation: **Open with Monitoring**; be prepared to pivot to a **2-hour delay** if 5:00–5:45 AM route checks find widespread glaze or drifting on bus-route connectors.
Run transportation/admin spot checks 5:00–5:30 AM focused on: bridges/overpasses, shaded curves, hill routes, subdivision entrances, and open north–south rural stretches prone to drifting.

Decision triggers for delay/closure: (a) verified active freezing drizzle or a uniform glaze on untreated surfaces, (b) repeated bus traction issues on multiple routes, (c) any building without power/heat by ~5:30–6:00 AM with no credible restoration before start time.
Because Rockford has had tech/communications fragility (per news intel), pre-stage a simple communications plan (SMS/robocall/social media cross-post) in case systems are degraded.
for_residents: Plan as if school is ON, but have a backup for a possible early-morning delay announcement.
Assume sidewalks/driveways may be slick: slow down, use traction, and give extra time for bus-stop walks.
Dress for a cold, breezy bus-stop wait (wind chills near/around 0°F possible).
for_authorities: Prioritize early salting on bridges/overpasses, intersections, and known drift-prone open stretches before 6 AM.
If wind remains gusty overnight, consider an extra pass on exposed north–south segments where drifting re-covers lanes after plows pass.
Coordinate any overnight outage restoration priorities near schools/major bus corridors given teens temperatures and building heat-up time.

positions.rationale:
Meteorologically, Tuesday 2026-03-17 looks like a post‑frontal cold, windy cleanup day rather than an active storm day during the school decision window. The hourly forecast concentrates the last measurable snow at 12:00 AM with only ~0.1" additional accumulation and then goes dry through the morning commute. With 6–8+ hours for treatment/plowing and improving visibility, a full closure is not the most likely outcome.

However, I’m not as low as 5% because: (1) the NWS has a Winter Weather Advisory headline continuing into Tuesday late morning (operationally, that often implies lingering travel hazards), and (2) very cold temps mean any residual moisture becomes persistent black ice, and even a *trace* freezing drizzle (suggested by the day condition label) can be a disproportionate disruptor. Net: modest closure risk, higher delay risk, dominated by residual/secondary-road icing rather than new snow totals.
keyFactors: **Timing:** Any measurable precip is front‑loaded near/around **12:00 AM Tue (3/17)** with ~0.02" QPF and ~0.28 cm (~0.11") snow, then **dry the rest of the night/morning** in the hourly feed—this strongly favors *open* or at most a delay.

**Cold lock-in:** Temps stay in the **mid‑teens to low‑20s°F** all day Tue, so whatever slush/compaction exists from Mon won’t melt; risk shifts from “new snow” to **black ice / packed snow** on untreated surfaces.

**Wind/drifting:** WNW winds still **15–20 mph** overnight/morning can keep **localized drifting** going if there’s loose snow left from Monday’s event, but speeds are below classic blizzard/whiteout territory for the Tue AM bus window.
**Visibility:** Brief dip to ~**1 mile** around midnight (light snow), improving to **6 miles** by daybreak—commute impacts more likely from *slickness* than visibility.

**Advisory signal vs hourly mismatch:** Day-summary says “light freezing rain” with high precip chance, yet hourly precip is basically gone after 00–01Z local. That mismatch is the main uncertainty: a **trace glaze/freezing drizzle near dawn** would increase closure odds despite low QPF.

positions.rationale: Putting the peer inputs together, this looks like a classic “tail-end” event: rougher weather on Monday (3/16) with improving conditions overnight into Tuesday (3/17). In that pattern, Rockford-area districts historically lean toward opening (sometimes with a delay) because crews can treat roads and the morning weather is not actively deteriorating. I’m slightly higher than the met team’s 5–10% because (1) the advisory language extends into Tuesday late morning and (2) the freezing-rain label + cold temps creates a non-trivial chance of a surprise glaze on secondary roads/bridges at 6–7 AM. Still, absent evidence of ongoing precip or a confirmed icing signal near daybreak, a full snow day remains unlikely.
keyFactors: Historical late-season behavior (mid‑March): districts in West Michigan typically stay open unless there is (a) meaningful snow during the 5–8 AM decision window or (b) verified, widespread icing. This forecast has neither strongly indicated.

Timing favors operations: the only meaningful precip signal in the hourly feed is around 12:00 AM, then conditions are dry/improving through the morning commute—historically that’s a low full-closure setup because road crews have a long treatment window.
Amounts are trace: forecast totalsnow ~0.28 cm (~0.1 in). Snow-only impacts at that level rarely produce a full cancellation in Kent County.

Wild-card hazard is ice, not snow: the day summary says “Light freezing rain” with high daily chance of precip (84%), but the hour-by-hour is basically dry after ~1 AM. Historically, when that label mismatch corresponds to *actual* pre-dawn freezing drizzle/glaze on untreated roads/bridges, closures/delays spike.

Carryover from Monday matters: Monday has wind + blowing snow and advisories; that can leave drifting/packed snow on neighborhood streets and bus-route connectors that stays slick in teens. This pushes toward delay risk more than full closure.

Non-weather operational risk: elevated (but not localized) power-outage context + cold temps increases the chance of a building-heat or traffic-signal issue, but we lack Rockford-specific outage confirmation—so it’s a modest upward nudge, not a decisive driver.
Derived from primary analysis

positions.rationale: From a news/operations standpoint, the strongest closure signal is the combination of an NWS advisory extending into Tuesday late morning plus Monday’s high-wind/winter impacts that can leave lingering travel hazards and scattered outages. Against that, multiple expert views (meteorology/history/infrastructure) converge on **little additional precip during the 5–8 AM window** and improving conditions, which in West Michigan usually produces *normal operations or at most a delay*. Given the lack of confirmed neighboring-district closures in the provided intel and no official Rockford statement yet, I land at a **low-but-not-negligible** snow-day risk driven mostly by (a) residual road conditions/ice on secondary routes and (b) any localized outage/heating issues at buildings.
keyFactors: No district closure/delay announcement found yet for Tuesday 2026-03-17 (typical if conditions are trending manageable and/or decision not made yet).

NWS Grand Rapids has a Winter Weather Advisory that explicitly runs until **Tue 11:00 AM EDT** for the area, which is a meaningful *institutional* signal that the Tuesday morning commute could still be impacted (even if new accumulation is light).

The forecast for Tuesday itself shows minimal *new* snow/precip after ~12–1 AM, which normally argues against a closure; however, cold temps in the mid-teens to low-20s lock in any leftover slush/packed snow and keep black-ice risk elevated at bus time.
Residual impacts from the Monday 2026-03-16 wind + snow event (drifting, snow-packed residential streets) can matter more than Tuesday’s incremental totals for bus-route safety.

Power/outage risk is the main non-meteorological “news” wildcard: statewide outages were elevated and wind advisories were in effect Monday; if north Kent/Rockford has notable overnight outages, closures become more likely even with a quiet Tuesday forecast.

positions.rationale:
Net meteorological read for Rockford, MI on Tue 2026-03-17 is a post-frontal cleanup day: very cold, breezy, but *not* an active heavy-precip morning. With the forecast concentrating the last measurable snow around midnight and showing improving visibility thereafter, widespread impassability by 6–8 AM is not the most likely outcome. 

I’m moving my closure probability slightly upward from the earlier ~12% to **15%** mainly to respect two uncertainties highlighted by peers: (1) the “light freezing rain” day-type label / high daily precip chance (84%) versus mostly-dry hourly list (potential for unmodeled trace freezing drizzle or lingering slickness), and (2) the Winter Weather Advisory language that explicitly runs into Tuesday late morning, which often correlates with lingering commute impacts even after the steadiest snow ends. Even so, given the tiny additional accumulation signal on Tuesday itself (~0.1"), my expectation remains ‘open, possibly delayed’ rather than ‘closed.’
keyFactors: **Little/no precip during the decision window (roughly 5–8 AM Tue 2026-03-17):** the hourly forecast shows the only measurable precip at ~12:00 AM (~0.02" liquid / ~0.1" snow), then dry the rest of the night and morning.

**Cold temps lock in hazards rather than create new ones:** temps stay in the mid-teens at bus time (15–16°F) with wind chills around ~0°F; that supports *refreeze/black ice persistence* on untreated surfaces, but doesn’t meet typical extreme-cold closure thresholds.

**Wind decreases vs Monday:** max wind Tue ~20 mph (gusts ~25 mph at midnight), substantially less than the Monday wind advisory conditions. That reduces the probability of widespread blowing/drifting *during* the AM commute.

**Advisory timing mismatch / lingering-hazard signal:** NWS Winter Weather Advisory messaging extends into Tue late morning, which is a non-trivial flag that travel impacts could linger even if new QPF is small. This is the main reason I’m not down at 5–10%.

**Primary-risk mechanism is ‘residual’ (not ‘active storm’):** packed snow, spotty drifting from Monday, and bridges/overpasses freezing. Those often yield delays or localized problems rather than districtwide closures in Kent County.

positions.rationale: Netting peer input with the historical lens: this looks like a classic “storm day Monday, cleanup Tuesday” setup. In Rockford/Kent County, cleanup days with *improving weather* and minimal new accumulation usually stay open; delays are more common than full cancellations. I’m nudging the closure probability slightly above our Round-1 ~12% because (a) the advisory narrative extends into Tuesday late morning (even if the hourly precip is light) and (b) the freezing-rain label/black-ice risk is exactly the kind of late-night-to-dawn wildcard that historically produces surprise calls. Still, absent evidence of active precip during the bus window, a full snow day remains unlikely.
keyFactors: **Historical pattern match favors “open (maybe delayed)”**: Late-season mid‑March cold shot with a short, overnight tail of light snow and improving conditions by daybreak typically produces *few full closures* in Kent County—especially when the last meaningful precip is around/just after midnight and crews have 6+ hours to treat.

**Totals are tiny for Tuesday**: Forecast Tuesday snow is ~0.1" with precip largely confined to ~12–1 AM; historically, that’s not enough by itself to cancel in Rockford-area districts unless roads were already in bad shape from the prior day.

**The real historical disruptor here is ice, not snow**: The “light freezing rain” daily label + teens temps creates a non-trivial chance of a *surprise trace glaze* (bridges/secondary roads). Trace ice events can spike cancellations despite low QPF—*but only if it’s present at 5–7 AM* (not just in the label).

**Carryover from Monday matters**: Monday’s wind + advisory conditions can leave drifting/packed snow on neighborhood streets; in this pattern, districts more often choose a **delay** than a full closure unless there’s widespread impassability.

**Decision threshold in mid‑March is slightly higher**: Districts are less eager to burn a closure day late-season unless hazards are widespread/verified (bus test drives reporting ice, multi-vehicle crashes, widespread outages).

positions.rationale:
I’m lowering the prior Safety-round 1 position (40%) because the provided hourly forecast for Tue 2026-03-17 shows **no ongoing precip during the 5–8 AM decision window** and improving visibility, which usually allows road commissions time to treat main routes. That reduces the likelihood of a *districtwide* closure.

I’m still materially higher than the ~12% cluster (meteorology/history/news) because from a safety standpoint the *highest-consequence* scenario here is a surprise **trace freezing drizzle / glaze** on top of cold pavement and residual snowpack—something the day-level ‘light freezing rain’ signal keeps on the table even if totals are small. Add in the lingering impacts of Monday’s wind/snow and the non-zero chance of localized outages/heat issues, and I see a moderate tail risk that could push administrators to close rather than run buses on patchy-glazed subdivision streets.

Net: most likely outcome is open (possibly with extra caution), next most likely is a delay, and a full closure is possible but not the base case.
keyFactors: Primary safety hazard is not snowfall depth but *ice/traction*: temps stay well below freezing all night (mid-teens °F), so any meltwater/slush from Monday refreezes into black ice—especially on bridges, shaded curves, and subdivision streets used by bus routes.

Forecast messaging conflict increases operational uncertainty: day summary flags **light freezing rain** with high precip chance, while hourly shows precip essentially ending right after midnight. If even a thin glaze redevelops 4–8 AM, risk escalates disproportionately versus the tiny QPF.

Wind/cleanup context: Monday’s strong winds + snow can leave localized drifting and unevenly treated roads; with Tuesday morning wind chills near ~0°F, there’s little passive melt to improve untreated spots before buses.

Outage/heat risk is a secondary but real safety driver: statewide outages elevated and cold temps mean even a modest local outage can prevent buildings from being safely occupied or adequately heated by opening time.

However, the *timing* favors staying open: worst visibility/precip is around 12–1 AM, then conditions improve through the commute window, which typically supports “open or delay” rather than full closure.

positions.rationale: From the NEWS/operations lens, I’m anchoring on *decision signals* rather than raw meteorology: (1) no official district announcement found yet (as of Mon 3/16 afternoon) argues against a planned closure; (2) advisory language + Monday impacts keep the issue “live” into Tue morning, which can tip administrators toward a delay if road/ice or outages persist; (3) given peers’ meteorology/history/infrastructure consensus that Tue morning conditions should be improving with minimal new accumulation, a full closure still looks unlikely, but not negligible due to the outage/ice wildcards. Net: I’m slightly higher than my Round 1 (12%) to reflect the Safety agent’s concern and the operational reality that *ice/outages* can override low snow totals.
keyFactors: No confirmed Rockford Public Schools closure/delay announcement found yet for Tue 2026-03-17; operationally that usually correlates with “open unless conditions worsen overnight.”

The forecast signal for *Tuesday morning (bus window)* is not an active heavy-snow event: precip in the WeatherAPI hourly feed is essentially confined to ~12:00 AM, then dry with improving visibility by daybreak. That reduces “news-style” closure momentum.

However, the NWS Winter Weather Advisory messaging (Kent County) extends into Tuesday late morning, which can drive preemptive decisions if roads are still hazardous at 5–7 AM (especially secondary/residential routes).

Power/outage wildcard: Monday’s wind advisory + tree/line damage risk plus very cold temps (teens) means even scattered outages at schools or along key traffic corridors can force closure (heating/lighting/traffic signals), even if snow totals are modest.

Forecast labeling mismatch (“light freezing rain” day summary vs mostly-dry Tuesday hours) leaves a small but meaningful chance of surprise freezing drizzle / glaze near dawn—high impact, low probability.

alert.headline: Winter Weather Advisory issued March 16 at 7:56AM EDT until March 17 at 11:00AM EDT by NWS Grand Rapids MI
alert.desc:
* WHAT...Snow accumulations between 1 and 3 inches with winds
gusting as high as 45 mph.

* WHERE...Barry and Kent Counties.

* WHEN...Until 11 AM EDT Tuesday.

* IMPACTS...Plan on slippery road conditions. The hazardous
conditions could impact the Monday morning and evening commutes.
Gusty winds could bring down tree branches.
alert.instruction: Slow down and use caution while traveling.

alert.headline: Wind Advisory issued March 16 at 3:16AM EDT until March 16 at 11:00PM EDT by NWS Grand Rapids MI
alert.areas: Mason; Lake; Oceana; Newaygo; Muskegon; Ottawa; Kent
alert.desc:
* WHAT...Northwest winds 15 to 30 mph with gusts up to 55 mph.

* WHERE...Kent, Ottawa, Lake, Mason, Muskegon, Newaygo, and Oceana
Counties.

* WHEN...Until 11 PM EDT this evening.

* IMPACTS...Gusty winds will blow around unsecured objects. Tree
limbs could be blown down and a few power outages may result.
alert.instruction:
Winds this strong can make driving difficult, especially for high
profile vehicles. Use extra caution.
